pages:
Lecture 3 ( Theory)
 Breast cancer 
Assist Lectu . Saja  K. Jassim, MSC, Adult Nursing
 University of Basrah 
College of Nursing 
Fundamentals of Nursing Department
Adult Nursing( 1) 2nd Stage

Lecture Contents
’üĄAnatomy and Physiology
’üĄIntroduction
’üĄSign and symptoms. 
’üĄMethod of diagnosis.
’üĄMedical Management
’üĄNursing Management

Anatomy and Physiology
1.Chest wall.
2.Pectoral muscles.
3.Lobules (glands that 
make milk).
4.Nipple surface.
5.Areola.    
6.Lactiferous duct tube 
that carries milk to the 
nipple   
7.Fatty tissue.   
8.Skin.
Lymphatic drainage of the breast

Breast Tumors
Malignant (Cancerous) 
Benign (Not ŌĆōCancerous)

Benign Tumors
’üĄNot  cancerous .
’üĄBenign  breast  tumors  are abnormal  
growths,  but they do not spread  outside  of 
the breast  and they are not life threatening .
’üĄMost  lumps  are caused  by the combination  
of cysts  and fibrosis  
’üĄCysts  are fluid -filled  sacs. 
’üĄFibrosis  is the formation  of scar -like 
tissue . 
’üĄThese  changes  can cause  breast  swelling  
and pain .

Breast cancer
’üĄBreast  cancer  is a malignant  (cancerous)  tumor  
that starts  in the cells of the breast . It is found  
mostly  in women , but men can get breast  
cancer,  too.

Types of Breast Cancer
Ductal 
Carcinoma
ŌĆóInvasive Ductal 
Carcinoma 
ŌĆóDuctal Carcinoma in situ 
(DCIS) Inflammatory 
Breast CancerLobular 
Carcinoma
ŌĆóInvasive Lobular 
Carcinoma
ŌĆóLobular Carcinoma in 
situ (LCIS)

Inflammatory Breast Cancer (IBC)
Ō¢¬Uncommon ( 1% to 3% of all breast 
cancers)
Ō¢¬Invasive Brest Cancer.
Ō¢¬No lump or tumor.
Ō¢¬Mistaken for infection in its early stages.
Ō¢¬IBC makes the skin of the breast look red 
and feel warm. 
Ō¢¬It also may make the skin look thick and 
pitted and may have an orange peel feel.
Ō¢¬The breast may get bigger, hard, tender, or 
itchy

Ductal Carcinoma in situ (DCIS) 
’üĄNon ŌĆōinvasive
’üĄcontained within the milk 
ducts .
’üĄMay become invasive 
(pre ŌĆōcancer).

Invasive (or Infiltrating) Ductal Carcinoma 
’üĄMost common breast cancer. 
’üĄAccounts for about 8 out of 10 invasive breast cancers.
’üĄ Lining of the ducts                         Grows /invades the breast 
tissues 
Spreads to lymph nodes                   Other organs

Lobular Carcinoma in situ (LCIS) 
’üĄNon ŌĆōInvasive.
’üĄContained in the lobules 
and does not spread to 
the tissues of the breast.
’üĄMay become malignant .

Risk factors 
1- Modifiable  factors  
2- Non- modifiable  factors

Method of diagnosis
’üĄDiagnostic tests and procedures for breast cancer 
include: 
’üĄBreast exam 
’üĄMammograms
’üĄBreast ultrasound                             Imaging tests
’üĄBreast MRI scan 
’üĄBiopsy

Breast Exam
’üĄClinical  Breast                             Breast  ŌĆōSelf Exam                                 
Exam  (CBE)                                           (BSE)

Breast imaging techniques
MammogramsBeast ultrasound
MRI Magnetic resonance imaging
Breast Biopsy

Treatment of breast Cancer Treatment of breast Cancer depend on various factors
’üĄThe type of breast cancer
’üĄThe stage and grade of the breast cancer
’üĄWhether or not the cancer cells are sensitive to hormones
’üĄThe patient's overall health
’üĄThe age of the patient
’üĄThe patient's own preferences

The main Treatment of breast Cancer Options may 
include
ŌØ¢Surgery
ŌØ¢Radiation therapy 
ŌØ¢Biological therapy (targeted drug therapy)
ŌØ¢Hormone therapy
ŌØ¢Chemotherapy

Surgery
Surgery for breast cancer:
’üĄLumpectomy 
’üĄMastectomy 
Lymph node surgery:
’üĄSentinel node biopsy 
’üĄAxillary lymph node dissection 
Breast reconstruction surgery

Lumpectomy 
’üĄBreast -conserving surgery (BCS)or partial/segmented 
mastectomy.
’üĄSurgically removing the tumor and a small margin of 
healthy tissue around it.
’üĄFollowed by radiation therapy

Mastectomy
’üĄSimple mastectomy : removing the lobules, ducts, fatty 
tissue, nipple, areola, and some skin.
’üĄModified radical mastectomy : simple mastectomy 
combined with the removal of the axillary lymph nodes .
’üĄRadical mastectomy : a simple mastectomy combined with 
removing the lymph nodes and muscles of the chest wall.
Mastectomy

Side effects of breast surgery
1.Pain.
2.Wound infection.  
3.(Lymphedema).If axillary lymph nodes are removed 
swelling of the arm and chest may occur

Lymph node surgery
1.Axillary  lymph  node  dissection :  about  10 to 40 lymph  nodes  are 
removed . 
2.Usually  done  at the same  time as the mastectomy  or breast -conserving  
surgery .
3.Sentinel  lymph  node  biopsy : is used to determine  if cancer  has spread  to 
the lymph  nodes  under  the arm without  removing   many  of them . 
4.A blue dye/radioactive  substance  is injected  in order  to identify  the 
sentinel  lymph  nodes  which  drains  lymph  from  the tumor . They  are then 
removed .

Side effect of lymph node surgery
1.Pain,  swelling,  bleeding,  and infection
2.Swelling  in the arm or chest  (Lymphedema) . 
3.Lymphedema  is mostly  due to axillary  lymph  node   biopsy .

Reconstruction breast or implant surgery
’üĄSurgical  procedures  aimed  at recreating  a breast  so that it looks  
as much  as possible  like the other  breast . 
’üĄThe surgeon  may use a breast  implant,  or tissue  from  another  part 
of the patient's  body .

Nursing Management
ŌØ¢Assessment  
’üĄThe health  history  is a valuable  tool to assess  the 
patient ŌĆÖs reaction  to the diagnosis  and her ability  to cope  
with it.

ŌØ¢Pre-operative Nursing Diagnoses
’üĄDe’¼ücient  knowledge  about  the planned  surgical  treatments  
’üĄAnxiety  related  to the diagnosis  of cancer  
’üĄFear related  to specific  treatments  and body  image  changes
’üĄRisk for ineffective coping (individual or family) related to the diagnosis of 
breast cancer and related treatment options

Postoperative Nursing Diagnoses
May include the following: 
’üĄPain and discomfort related to surgical procedure 
’üĄDisturbed sensory perception related to nerve irritation in affected arm, breast, 
or chest wall 
’üĄDisturbed body image related to loss or alteration of the breast 
’üĄSelf-care de’¼ücit related to partial immobility of upper extremity on operative 
side 
’üĄRisk for change in self -image, and fear of partner ŌĆÖs responses 
’üĄDe’¼ücient knowledge: arm exercises to regain mobility of affected extremity

ŌØ¢Planning and Goals
The major  goals  may include  
’üĄincreased  knowledge  about  the disease  and its treatment
’üĄ reduction  of preoperative  and postoperative  fear, anxiety,  and emotional  
stress
’üĄ improvement  of decision -making  ability
’üĄ pain management
’üĄ improvement  in coping  abilities
’üĄabsence  of complications .

ŌØ¢Nursing Interventions
Preoperative Nursing Interventions
’üĄProviding Education and Preparation about 
Surgical Treatments 
’üĄReducing Fear and Anxiety and Improving Coping 
Ability 
’üĄPromoting Decision -Making Ability

Postoperative Nursing Interventions
’üĄRelieving Pain and Discomfort
’üĄManaging Postoperative Sensations 
’üĄPromoting Positive Body Image 
’üĄPromoting Positive Adjustment and Coping 
’üĄMonitoring and Managing Potential Complications
’üĄPromoting Home and Community -Based Care (TEACHING 
PATIENTS SELF -CARE).

Radiation therapy for breast cancer
Ō¢¬Radiation  therapy  is treatment  with high-energy  rays (such  as x-rays)  or particles  to kill 
cancer  cells.
Ō¢¬The patient  may require  three  to five sessions  per week  for three  to six weeks .
Ō¢¬The type of breast  cancer  will determine  the type of radiation  therapy  used.
Side effects of radiation therapy  
1.Swelling and heaviness in the breast.
2.Sunburn -like changes in the skin  and feeling very tired. 
3.Weakness .
4.Damage some of the nerves to the arm. This can lead to numbness, pain, and weakness in the 
shoulder, arm and hand. 
5.Radiation to lymph nodes causes  (Lymphedema).

Chemotherapy
Ō¢¬Chemotherapy  (chemo)  is the use of cancer -killing  drugs .
Ō¢¬Intravenously,  given  as a shot,  or taken  as a pill or liquid . 
Ō¢¬They  enter  the bloodstream  and reach  most  parts  of the body .
Ō¢¬Combats  metastasis .
Ō¢¬Damage  some  normal  cells.
Side effects of chemotherapy  1.Hair loss
2.Loss  of appetite  or increased  appetite
3.Nausea  and vomiting
4.A higher  risk of infection  (low WBC  count)
5.Stopping  of menstrual  periods
6.Easy  bruising  or bleeding  (low platelets)
7.Being  very tired
8.Menstrual  changes : infertility
9.Nerve  damage : pain,  burning  or tingling  and sensitivity  to cold or hot.
10.Heart  damage

Hormonal therapy
’üĄUsed for breast cancers that are sensitive to hormones. 
’üĄThese types of cancer are often referred to as ER positive (estrogen 
receptor positive) and PR positive (progesterone receptor positive) 
cancers. Side effects of Hormonal therapy  
1.Mouth sores
2.Diarrhea
3.Nausea
4.Fatigue
5.Feeling weak or tired
6.Low blood counts
7.Shortness of breath
8.Cough

Summary
’üĄ Breast cancer is a malignant (cancerous) tumor that starts in the 
cells of the breast 
’üĄRisk factors of breast cancer are modifiable such as Diet, obesity, 
smoking, and non modifiable such as age, gender, genetic.
’üĄTreatment of breast CA. can be surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy

Lecture Questions
1. Which finding is considered abnormal when conducting an examination on a 68-year-old 
woman?
1. Dark pink areola
2. Pendulous breasts
3. Serous nipple drainage
4. Granular feeling when palpating the breast 
4. What is the reason for palpating axillary lymph nodes during a clinical breast 
examination?
1. Axillary nodes fluctuate during the month in response to the menstrual cycle.
2. Axillary node tenderness is the most common initial symptom of breast cancer.
3. The lymph network in the breast primarily drains toward the axillary lymph nodes.
4. This is a matter of convenience because of the close proximity of the axillae to the breasts.

Reference
Ō¢¬ Textbook of medical -surgical nursing Description: 
14th edition. | Philadelphia : Wolters  Kluwer, [ 2018 ], 
ISBN 9781496355157 (2-volume American edition)
THANKS

Radiation therapy for breast cancer
Ō¢¬Radiation  therapy  is treatment  with high-energy  rays (such  as x-rays)  or particles  to kill 
cancer  cells.
Ō¢¬The patient  may require  three  to five sessions  per week  for three  to six weeks .
Ō¢¬The type of breast  cancer  will determine  the type of radiation  therapy  used.
Side effects of radiation therapy  
1.Swelling and heaviness in the breast.
2.Sunburn -like changes in the skin  and feeling very tired. 
3.Weakness .
4.Damage some of the nerves to the arm. This can lead to numbness, pain, and weakness in the 
shoulder, arm and hand. 
5.Radiation to lymph nodes causes  (Lymphedema).

Chemotherapy
Ō¢¬Chemotherapy  (chemo)  is the use of cancer -killing  drugs .
Ō¢¬Intravenously,  given  as a shot,  or taken  as a pill or liquid . 
Ō¢¬They  enter  the bloodstream  and reach  most  parts  of the body .
Ō¢¬Combats  metastasis .
Ō¢¬Damage  some  normal  cells.
Side effects of chemotherapy  1.Hair loss
2.Loss  of appetite  or increased  appetite
3.Nausea  and vomiting
4.A higher  risk of infection  (low WBC  count)
5.Stopping  of menstrual  periods
6.Easy  bruising  or bleeding  (low platelets)
7.Being  very tired
8.Menstrual  changes : infertility
9.Nerve  damage : pain,  burning  or tingling  and sensitivity  to cold or hot.
10.Heart  damage

Hormonal therapy
’üĄUsed for breast cancers that are sensitive to hormones. 
’üĄThese types of cancer are often referred to as ER positive (estrogen 
receptor positive) and PR positive (progesterone receptor positive) 
cancers. Side effects of Hormonal therapy  
1.Mouth sores
2.Diarrhea
3.Nausea
4.Fatigue
5.Feeling weak or tired
6.Low blood counts
7.Shortness of breath
8.Cough

Summary
’üĄ Breast cancer is a malignant (cancerous) tumor that starts in the 
cells of the breast 
’üĄRisk factors of breast cancer are modifiable such as Diet, obesity, 
smoking, and non modifiable such as age, gender, genetic.
’üĄTreatment of breast CA. can be surgery, radiation, and chemotherapy
